title: Find first occurrence of a target
description: Find the first occurrence index of a target element in a sorted array using binary search

examples.explanation:
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1, 1≠2, continue
Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=2, 2=2, match found
Return index 1

Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1, 1≠4
Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=3, 3≠4
Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=5, 5≠4
Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=7, 7≠4
Step 5: i=4, arr[4]=9, 9≠4
End of array reached, return -1

approaches.name: Linear Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array from start to end, checking each element until the target is found, returning the first index. Use this only if the array is unsorted or as a baseline for small inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Linear Scan for First Occurrence in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of linear scan to find first occurrence index of target in sorted array
ladders.explanation: This full problem implements a linear scan approach to find the first occurrence of a target element in a sorted array. The intuition is straightforward: since we're scanning from the beginning, the first time we encounter the target will be its first occurrence. We iterate through each element sequentially, comparing it with the target. When we find a match, we immediately return that index. If we reach the end without finding the target, we return -1. The algorithm starts at index 0, checks each element, and stops at the first match. Time complexity is O(n) where n is array length, as we may need to scan all elements in worst case. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few variables. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) as the complete implementation that calls the scanning logic. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling empty arrays, 2) Continuing to search after finding first occurrence, 3) Off-by-one errors in loop conditions, 4) Not returning -1 when target not found. Avoid these by carefully defining loop boundaries and having clear termination conditions. Real code pattern: def find_first_occurrence(arr, target): for i in range(len(arr)): if arr[i] == target: return i return -1

examples.trace:
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1, 1≠2, continue
Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=2, 2=2, match found
Return index 1

Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1, 1≠4
Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=3, 3≠4
Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=5, 5≠4
Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=7, 7≠4
Step 5: i=4, arr[4]=9, 9≠4
End of array reached, return -1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Linear Element Search
ladders.desc: Core scanning function that searches for target in array
ladders.explanation: This key subroutine implements the linear search logic that scans through an array to find a target element. The intuition is simple: examine each element in order until we find what we're looking for. We maintain a current index pointer that starts at 0 and increments by 1 each iteration. At each position, we compare the array element with the target. If they match, we've found the target and return the current index. If we exhaust all elements without finding a match, we return a sentinel value (typically -1). The algorithm requires: 1) Initializing index to 0, 2) While index < array length: compare current element with target, 3) If equal: return index, 4) Else: increment index, 5) After loop: return -1. Time complexity is O(n) as we may check all n elements. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 as the core search logic that the full problem wraps with proper input validation and return handling. Common mistakes: 1) Using wrong comparison operator, 2) Forgetting to increment index, 3) Returning wrong value when not found. Avoid by writing clear loop invariants. Real code pattern: def linear_search(arr, target): i = 0; while i < len(arr): if arr[i] == target: return i; i += 1; return -1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sequential Comparison Loop
ladders.desc: The iterative process of comparing each element with target
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the sequential comparison loop that forms the heart of linear search. The intuition is that to find something in a list, you look at each item one by one until you find it. We use a loop to systematically visit each array position. At each iteration, we perform a comparison: is the current element equal to the target? If yes, we've succeeded and can stop. If no, we move to the next position. The loop continues until either we find the target or we've checked all positions. The algorithm: 1) Start with index = 0, 2) While index is valid (less than array length): perform comparison, 3) Based on result: either return success or increment index. Time complexity is linear in array size. Space is constant. This connects to L1 as the comparison loop that the search function organizes with proper initialization and return logic. Common mistakes: 1) Comparing with wrong element (off-by-one), 2) Not handling empty array case, 3) Infinite loop from incorrect increment. Avoid by clearly defining loop exit conditions. Real code pattern: i = 0; while i < n: if a[i] == target: found; else: i += 1

examples.trace:
Iteration 1: i=0, arr[0]=3≠7, i=1
Iteration 2: i=1, arr[1]=7=7, found

Iteration 1: i=0, arr[0]=4≠20, i=1
Iteration 2: i=1, arr[1]=8≠20, i=2
Iteration 3: i=2, arr[2]=12≠20, i=3
Iteration 4: i=3, arr[3]=16≠20, i=4
i=4≥len(arr), not found

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing and Comparison
ladders.desc: Accessing array elements and comparing with target value
ladders.explanation: This building block combines array indexing with value comparison, which is fundamental to linear search. The intuition is that to check if an element matches our target, we need to: 1) Access the element at a specific position, 2) Compare it with our target value. Array indexing allows us to retrieve the element at position i using arr[i]. Comparison checks if two values are equal. We need to understand that array indices start at 0, so the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. The comparison operation returns true if values are equal, false otherwise. The algorithm: Given array arr, index i, and target: 1) Retrieve element = arr[i], 2) Compare element == target, 3) Return comparison result. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations. Space is O(1). This connects to L2 as the atomic operation performed at each iteration of the comparison loop. Common mistakes: 1) Using 1-based indexing, 2) Confusing assignment (=) with comparison (==), 3) Comparing wrong data types. Avoid by practicing array access patterns and understanding comparison operators. Real code pattern: element = arr[index]; if element == target: match = True; else: match = False

examples.trace:
Step 1: Access arr[1] = 20
Step 2: Compare 20 == 20
Step 3: Result is True (match)

Step 1: Access arr[2] = 25
Step 2: Compare 25 == 30
Step 3: Result is False (no match)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access
ladders.desc: Retrieving an element from an array using its index
ladders.explanation: This basic operation focuses on accessing array elements by index, which is fundamental to working with arrays. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we can retrieve any element if we know its position (index). Arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at position 0, second at position 1, etc. To access an element, we use the syntax arr[index], where arr is the array variable and index is an integer representing the position. The operation returns the value stored at that position. It's crucial that the index is within valid bounds: 0 ≤ index < length of array. Accessing outside these bounds causes an error. The algorithm: Given array arr and index i: 1) Check i is valid (0 ≤ i < len(arr)), 2) Compute memory offset, 3) Retrieve value at that position. Time complexity is O(1) - constant time access. Space is O(1). This connects to L3 as the first step in the indexing-comparison building block. Common mistakes: 1) Using 1 as first index, 2) Accessing index equal to array length (should be length-1), 3) Negative indices (unless language supports). Avoid by remembering zero-based indexing and checking bounds. Real code pattern: element = arr[index] (assuming valid index)

examples.trace:
Array has indices: 0→10, 1→20, 2→30
Access index 1: arr[1] = 20

Array indices: 0→5, 1→15, 2→25, 3→35
Access index 3: arr[3] = 35

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Value Comparison
ladders.desc: Determining if two values are equal
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers value comparison, specifically checking if two values are equal. The intuition is simple: we often need to know if two things are the same. In programming, we use the equality operator (== in most languages) to compare two values. The operator returns True if the values are equal, False otherwise. It's important to distinguish between assignment (=) which sets a value, and comparison (==) which checks equality. Comparison works for various data types: integers, floats, strings, etc., though floating-point comparisons require care due to precision. The algorithm: Given two values a and b: 1) Evaluate a == b, 2) Result is True if equal, False otherwise. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L4 as the comparison operation used after accessing an array element. Common mistakes: 1) Using = instead of ==, 2) Comparing incompatible types, 3) Not understanding that == compares values, not identities. Avoid by practicing with different value types and remembering that == is for comparison. Real code pattern: result = (value1 == value2)

examples.trace:
Compare 5 == 5
Both are integer 5
Values are equal, result is True

Compare 7 == 3
7 and 3 are different values
Result is False

approaches.name: Standard Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Perform binary search to find any occurrence of the target, then scan leftwards linearly to locate the first occurrence. This combines logarithmic search with linear verification for duplicates.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n + k) where k is the number of duplicates

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find First Occurrence with Binary Search + Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Implement complete solution: binary search to find any target occurrence, then linear left scan to find first occurrence.
ladders.explanation: This approach efficiently solves the problem by leveraging the sorted nature of the array. The intuition is that binary search gives us O(log n) time to locate ANY occurrence of the target, but due to potential duplicates, we need to find the FIRST occurrence. After binary search finds an index where the target exists, we perform a linear scan leftwards until we find where the target begins. This works because the array is sorted, so all duplicates are contiguous. The algorithm: 1) Perform standard binary search to find any index where arr[mid] == target. 2) If found, initialize result = mid. 3) While result > 0 and arr[result-1] == target: decrement result. 4) Return result. If not found, return -1. Time complexity is O(log n + k) where k is number of duplicates (worst-case O(n) if all elements are target). Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by using binary search as the core subroutine. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle empty array, not checking bounds during left scan, returning the binary search index without verifying it's the first occurrence. Real pattern: def first_occurrence(arr, target): low, high = 0, len(arr)-1; while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] == target: result = mid; while result > 0 and arr[result-1] == target: result -= 1; return result; elif arr[mid] < target: low = mid+1; else: high = mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: Binary search: low=0, high=5, mid=2 → arr[2]=2 == target → found at index 2. Linear left scan: result=2, check arr[1]=2 == target → result=1, check arr[0]=1 != target → stop. Return 1.

Binary search: low=0, high=5, mid=2 → arr[2]=7 < 8 → low=3; low=3, high=5, mid=4 → arr[4]=8 == target → found at index 4. Linear left scan: result=4, check arr[3]=8 == target → result=3, check arr[2]=7 != target → stop. Return 3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search to Find Any Occurrence
ladders.desc: Implement binary search to locate any index where element equals target in sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This is the critical subroutine that enables efficient search. The intuition: because the array is sorted, we can repeatedly halve the search space by comparing the middle element to the target. If the middle equals target, we found an occurrence. If middle is less than target, the target must be in the right half (if present). If middle is greater, target must be in the left half. This reduces the problem size by half each iteration, giving O(log n) time. Algorithm: 1) Initialize low=0, high=len(arr)-1. 2) While low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2. 3) If arr[mid] == target: return mid. 4) Else if arr[mid] < target: low = mid+1. 5) Else: high = mid-1. 6) Return -1 if loop ends. Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the index to start the left scan. Common mistakes: using < instead of <= in loop condition (misses single-element cases), integer overflow in mid calculation (use low + (high-low)//2), not updating bounds correctly. Real pattern: def binary_search(arr, target): low, high = 0, len(arr)-1; while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return mid; elif arr[mid] < target: low = mid+1; else: high = mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: low=0, high=4, mid=2 → arr[2]=5 == target → return 2.
low=0, high=4, mid=2 → arr[2]=6 < 7 → low=3; low=3, high=4, mid=3 → arr[3]=8 > 7 → high=2; low=3 > high=2 → exit loop, return -1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Halving Search Space with Comparisons
ladders.desc: Given sorted array and target, repeatedly eliminate half of remaining elements based on comparison.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of binary search. The intuition: in a sorted array, comparing any element to the target tells us which half of the array to search next. If arr[mid] < target, then target (if present) must be to the right because all elements left of mid are ≤ arr[mid] < target. Similarly, if arr[mid] > target, target must be to the left. This allows us to discard half the remaining elements with each comparison, leading to exponential reduction in search space. The process: start with entire array as search space [low, high]. Compute mid index. Compare arr[mid] to target. If equal, found. If less, set new low = mid+1 (search right half). If greater, set new high = mid-1 (search left half). Repeat until search space empty. Time: O(log n) as search space halves each step. Space: O(1). This connects to L1 as the mechanism inside the binary search loop. Common mistakes: including mid in new search space when it's already checked (leads to infinite loops), incorrect termination condition. Real pattern: while search_space_not_empty: mid = middle_index; if arr[mid] == target: found; elif arr[mid] < target: search_right_half; else: search_left_half.

examples.input: arr = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50], target = 40, current search space indices [0,4]
examples.trace: mid = (0+4)//2 = 2, arr[2]=30 < 40 → eliminate left half including mid → new low = mid+1 = 3, high remains 4 → search space [3,4].

mid = (0+4)//2 = 2, arr[2]=25 > 15 → eliminate right half including mid → new high = mid-1 = 1, low remains 0 → search space [0,1]; mid = (0+1)//2 = 0, arr[0]=5 < 15 → new low = mid+1 = 1, high remains 1 → search space [1,1]; mid = (1+1)//2 = 1, arr[1]=15 == target → found.

testCases.expected: Not found, space reduces to empty

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Elements and Updating Bounds
ladders.desc: Given current search bounds [low, high] and mid index, compare arr[mid] to target and compute new bounds.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept handles the decision logic at each binary search step. The intuition: after computing mid index, we need to determine whether the target could be in the left or right portion based on the sorted order. If arr[mid] equals target, we're done. If arr[mid] < target, since array is sorted ascending, all elements with indices ≤ mid have values ≤ arr[mid], which is < target, so they cannot be the target. Thus, we can discard left half including mid, setting low = mid+1. If arr[mid] > target, all elements with indices ≥ mid have values ≥ arr[mid] > target, so discard right half including mid, setting high = mid-1. This updates the search space accurately. The operation: compare arr[mid] and target, then adjust low or high accordingly. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L2 as the core action within the halving process. Common mistakes: setting low = mid or high = mid (including checked element), forgetting the sorted assumption. Real pattern: if arr[mid] == target: return mid; elif arr[mid] < target: low = mid + 1; else: high = mid - 1.

examples.trace: arr[2]=30, compare: 30 < 40 → target in right half → discard indices 0..2 → set low = mid+1 = 3, high unchanged = 4.
arr[2]=25, compare: 25 > 15 → target in left half → discard indices 2..4 → set high = mid-1 = 1, low unchanged = 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Mid Index and Access Array Element
ladders.desc: Given low and high indices, compute middle index and retrieve the element at that position.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is essential for binary search. The intuition: to divide the search space, we need to examine the middle element. Computing mid index as the average of low and high gives us the center position. Then accessing arr[mid] provides the value to compare against the target. For mid calculation: mid = (low + high) // 2 using integer division to get floor value. This ensures mid is an integer index within bounds [low, high]. Accessing arr[mid] is a constant-time array lookup. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L3 as the first step before comparison. Common mistakes: using floating-point division (need integer index), potential integer overflow with large indices (use low + (high-low)//2), accessing out-of-bounds if indices invalid. Real pattern: mid = (low + high) // 2; element = arr[mid].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Integer Division
ladders.desc: Access array elements by index and perform integer arithmetic to compute positions.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite for binary search. Arrays are data structures where elements are stored in contiguous memory locations and accessed via indices starting from 0. To get an element, use arr[index] where index is an integer within 0 to len(arr)-1. Integer division (// in Python) divides two integers and returns the floor result as an integer, discarding any remainder. For example, 7 // 2 = 3. This is used to compute middle indices. The intuition: binary search needs to calculate positions mathematically, and integer division ensures we get valid integer indices. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L4 as the basic computation. Common mistakes: using / which gives float, misunderstanding zero-based indexing, accessing out-of-range indices. Real pattern: index = 2; value = arr[index]; mid = (a + b) // 2.

examples.trace: Array indices: 0→10, 1→20, 2→30, 3→40. arr[2] returns element at position 2: 30.
0 + 3 = 3; integer division 3 // 2 = 1 (since 2 fits into 3 once with remainder 1, floor is 1).

approaches.name: Modified Binary Search for First Occurrence O(log n)
approaches.summary: Adapt binary search to always move left when the target is found or when mid element is greater than or equal to the target, ensuring the first occurrence is identified directly without extra scans. This is optimal for sorted arrays with duplicates.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find First Occurrence of Target in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of modified binary search to find the first occurrence index of a target element in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the first occurrence of a target value in a sorted array that may contain duplicates. The naive approach would be linear scan O(n), but we can achieve O(log n) using a modified binary search. The key insight is that when we find the target at mid position, we cannot immediately return it because there might be earlier occurrences to the left. Instead, we continue searching in the left half. Similarly, when arr[mid] >= target, we move left; otherwise, we move right. This ensures we converge to the first occurrence. We maintain a result variable that gets updated whenever we find the target, and finally return it after the search completes. The algorithm initializes left=0, right=n-1, result=-1. While left <= right: calculate mid = left + (right-left)//2. If arr[mid] == target: update result=mid and search left (right=mid-1). Else if arr[mid] > target: search left (right=mid-1). Else: search right (left=mid+1). Return result. Time: O(log n) as we halve search space each iteration. Space: O(1) using only pointers. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of moving left when target found. Common mistakes: returning mid immediately when target found (misses earlier duplicates), incorrect termination condition (should be left <= right), integer overflow in mid calculation (use left+(right-left)//2). Real pattern: while left <= right: mid = left + (right-left)//2; if arr[mid] >= target: right = mid-1; if arr[mid] == target: result = mid; else: left = mid+1.

examples.trace: Initial: left=0, right=6, result=-1. Iter1: mid=3 (arr[3]=2) == target → result=3, right=2. Iter2: left=0, right=2, mid=1 (arr[1]=2) == target → result=1, right=0. Iter3: left=0, right=0, mid=0 (arr[0]=1) < target → left=1. Iter4: left=1, right=0 → loop ends. Return result=1.
Initial: left=0, right=4, result=-1. Iter1: mid=2 (arr[2]=5) < target → left=3. Iter2: left=3, right=4, mid=3 (arr[3]=7) > target → right=2. Iter3: left=3, right=2 → loop ends. Return result=-1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Move Left When Target Found or Greater/Equal
ladders.desc: The core decision logic that determines when to search left versus right in the modified binary search.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the modified binary search for first occurrence. In standard binary search, when arr[mid] == target, we return mid immediately. Here, we need to continue searching left to find potentially earlier occurrences. The decision rule: if arr[mid] >= target, we move left (right = mid-1); otherwise, we move right (left = mid+1). Additionally, when arr[mid] == target, we record this position as a candidate result. The intuition: if arr[mid] is greater than target, the first occurrence must be to the left. If arr[mid] equals target, the first occurrence could be at mid or further left. If arr[mid] is less than target, the first occurrence must be to the right. This ensures we narrow down to the leftmost target. Time: O(1) per comparison. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This decision logic is executed in each iteration of the binary search loop. Common mistakes: using > instead of >= (misses equal case), not recording result when target found. Real pattern: if arr[mid] >= target: right = mid-1; if arr[mid] == target: result = mid; else: left = mid+1.

examples.input: arr = [1,2,2,2,3], left=0, right=4, mid=2, target=2, result=-1
examples.trace: At mid=2, arr[2]=2 == target → result=2, and since arr[mid] >= target, we move left: right=mid-1=1. left remains 0.

examples.input: arr = [1,2,2,2,3], left=0, right=1, mid=0, target=2, result=2
examples.trace: At mid=0, arr[0]=1 < target → we move right: left=mid+1=1. right remains 1, result unchanged.

testCases.input: arr = [1,2,3,4,5], left=0, right=4, mid=2, target=3, result=-1
arr = [1,2,3,4,5], left=0, right=4, mid=2, target=5, result=-1
arr = [2,2,2,2], left=0, right=3, mid=1, target=2, result=-1
arr = [1,3,5], left=0, right=2, mid=1, target=2, result=-1
arr = [1,3,5], left=0, right=2, mid=1, target=3, result=-1
arr = [1,3,5], left=0, right=2, mid=1, target=4, result=-1
arr = [10,20,30], left=0, right=2, mid=1, target=20, result=-1
arr = [10,20,30], left=0, right=2, mid=1, target=10, result=-1
arr = [5,7,9], left=0, right=2, mid=1, target=7, result=-1
arr = [5,7,9], left=0, right=2, mid=1, target=6, result=-1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Converging to Leftmost Target by Halving Search Space
ladders.desc: The technique of repeatedly narrowing the search interval to zero in on the first occurrence.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves maintaining a search interval [left, right] and updating it based on comparisons to converge to the first occurrence. We start with the entire array. At each step, we check the middle element. If it's greater than or equal to the target, we discard the right half (including mid) because the first occurrence cannot be after mid. If it's less than the target, we discard the left half (excluding mid) because the first occurrence must be after mid. This halving continues until the interval is empty. The key is that even when we find the target, we continue searching left, ensuring we don't miss earlier duplicates. The intuition is similar to binary search but with a twist: we don't stop at equality. Time: O(log n) as interval halves each time. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This logic uses the decision subroutine to update left/right. Common mistakes: not updating bounds correctly (off-by-one), infinite loops if termination condition wrong. Real pattern: while left <= right: update mid; if arr[mid] >= target: right = mid-1; else: left = mid+1.

examples.trace: Start: [0,4]. Iter1: mid=2 (arr[2]=2) >= target → interval [0,1]. Iter2: mid=0 (arr[0]=1) < target → interval [1,1]. Iter3: mid=1 (arr[1]=2) >= target → interval [1,0] (empty).
Start: [0,4]. Iter1: mid=2 (arr[2]=7) >= target → interval [0,1]. Iter2: mid=0 (arr[0]=5) >= target → interval [0,-1] (empty).

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Search on Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Standard binary search algorithm to find any occurrence of a target in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: Binary search is an efficient algorithm for finding an element in a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. It works by comparing the target to the middle element. If they match, return the index. If the target is less, search the left half; if greater, search the right half. This reduces the problem size by half each step, leading to O(log n) time. The intuition relies on the sorted property: all elements to the left are smaller, all to the right are larger. We maintain pointers left and right defining the current interval. Calculate mid = left + (right-left)//2 to avoid overflow. Continue while left <= right. This building block is essential because our modified version adapts it for first occurrence. Time: O(log n). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This is the foundation we modify by changing the equality handling. Common mistakes: incorrect mid calculation (use integer division), off-by-one in termination (left <= right ensures all elements checked). Real pattern: while left <= right: mid = left + (right-left)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return mid; elif arr[mid] < target: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: Start: left=0, right=4. Iter1: mid=2, arr[2]=5 == target → return 2.
Start: left=0, right=4. Iter1: mid=2, arr[2]=6 < target → left=3. Iter2: left=3, right=4, mid=3, arr[3]=8 > target → right=2. Iter3: left=3, right=2 → loop ends, return -1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Update Pointers in Array
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of comparing array elements and adjusting search boundaries.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing an array element at a given index, comparing it to a target value, and updating left/right pointers accordingly. In array indexing, we use zero-based indices: arr[i] gives the element at position i. Comparison operators (==, <, >) determine the relationship. Based on the comparison, we update left or right pointer to narrow the search interval. For example, if arr[mid] < target, we set left = mid+1 to exclude the left half. This operation is performed in constant time O(1). The intuition is that by moving pointers, we effectively discard parts of the array that cannot contain the target, leveraging the sorted order. Space: O(1) for the pointers. Connection to L3: This operation is repeated in each iteration of binary search. Common mistakes: updating left=mid instead of mid+1 (may cause infinite loop), incorrect array access (index out of bounds). Real pattern: mid = (left+right)//2; if arr[mid] < target: left = mid+1; elif arr[mid] > target: right = mid-1.

examples.trace: Calculate mid=1 (arr[1]=20). Since 20 < 25, update left=mid+1=2. right remains 3.
Calculate mid=1 (arr[1]=10). Since 10 == target, in standard binary search we'd return, but for pointer update: if treating as > case, right=mid-1=0; left remains 0.

testCases.expected: left=0, right=0 (if arr[mid] == target, update as >)
left=0, right=0 (if arr[mid] == target, update as >)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sorted Arrays and Element Access
ladders.desc: Understanding sorted arrays and how to access elements by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basics of sorted arrays and indexing. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations. A sorted array means the elements are arranged in non-decreasing order (ascending). For example, [1,3,5,7] is sorted because each element is greater than or equal to the previous one. We access elements using zero-based indices: the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. For array arr of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. Accessing arr[i] takes O(1) time. The sorted property is crucial for binary search because it allows us to make decisions based on comparisons: if arr[mid] < target, we know all elements to the left are also < target. This concept is prerequisite for any search algorithm on sorted data. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(n) for storing the array. Connection to L4: This enables the compare operation by providing ordered elements. Common mistakes: assuming 1-based indexing, accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real pattern: arr = [1,2,3]; first element = arr[0]=1, second = arr[1]=2.

examples.trace: Length=5, indices 0-4. arr[0]=2, arr[1]=4, arr[2]=6, arr[3]=8, arr[4]=10. Sorted because 2<4<6<8<10.
Length=4, indices 0-3. arr[0]=5, arr[1]=5, arr[2]=5, arr[3]=5. Sorted (non-decreasing) because 5<=5<=5<=5.
